locked_facts: Viscosity is internal friction in fluids — resistance to flow between adjacent layers
Newton's law of viscosity: F/A = η(dv/dy) — shear stress = viscosity × velocity gradient
Stokes' law: drag force on sphere F = 6πηrv
Terminal velocity: v_t = 2r²(ρ_s - ρ_f)g/(9η) — when drag + buoyancy = weight
Terminal velocity ∝ r² — larger spheres fall faster in viscous fluid
Reynolds number Re = ρvd/η determines laminar (Re < 2000) vs turbulent flow
Viscosity of liquids decreases with temperature; viscosity of gases increases with temperature
truth_statements: Viscosity is the property of a fluid that opposes relative motion between adjacent fluid layers
Stokes' law gives the viscous drag force on a small sphere moving slowly through a viscous fluid as F = 6πηrv
Terminal velocity is reached when the net force on the falling body becomes zero — weight equals drag plus buoyancy
Terminal velocity is proportional to the square of the radius — a sphere twice as large falls four times faster
The Reynolds number determines whether flow is laminar (smooth) or turbulent (chaotic)

aha_moment.what_to_show: A steel ball falling through glycerine: initially accelerates (weight > drag), drag increases with speed, until drag + buoyancy = weight → constant terminal velocity. A velocity vs time graph shows the curve plateauing. Larger ball reaches higher terminal velocity.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Velocity-time graph rising and plateauing, with force arrows showing equilibrium at terminal velocity
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think objects accelerate indefinitely in fluids — seeing the velocity plateau and force balance at terminal velocity is the key insight

simulation_states.label: Viscosity concept
simulation_states.description: Two fluid layers sliding past each other. Viscous force resists relative motion. Higher η = more resistance. Water vs honey comparison.
simulation_states.emphasis: Viscosity is internal friction — thicker fluids have higher viscosity

simulation_states.label: Stokes' law
simulation_states.description: A small sphere falling through viscous fluid. Drag F = 6πηrv increases with speed. Three forces: weight (down), buoyancy (up), drag (up).
simulation_states.emphasis: Drag increases linearly with velocity — the faster it moves, the more the fluid pushes back

simulation_states.label: Terminal velocity
simulation_states.description: As sphere falls, drag increases until W = F_b + F_drag. Net force = 0 → constant velocity. v_t = 2r²(ρ_s-ρ_f)g/(9η). Larger sphere → higher v_t (∝ r²).
simulation_states.emphasis: At terminal velocity: no net force, constant speed. v_t ∝ r².

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts sphere radius, density, and fluid viscosity. Terminal velocity and settling time update. v-t graph shown. Reynolds number indicator: laminar or turbulent.
simulation_states.emphasis: Double the radius → 4× terminal velocity. Increase viscosity → slower fall.

common_misconceptions.belief: Terminal velocity means maximum possible velocity
common_misconceptions.correction: Terminal velocity is the CONSTANT velocity reached when drag equals weight (net force = 0). It's not the maximum the object COULD reach — it's the speed at which acceleration becomes zero in a viscous medium.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show acceleration dropping to zero at terminal velocity — object continues at constant speed, not accelerating

common_misconceptions.belief: Heavier objects always have higher terminal velocity
common_misconceptions.correction: Terminal velocity depends on density difference, radius, AND viscosity. A large light ball can have higher terminal velocity than a small heavy ball because v_t ∝ r².
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show a large light sphere reaching higher v_t than a small dense sphere
